retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis paradigm may shift significantly with the accelerated advancements in medical science and technology. Emerging treatments, potentially involving neuroprotective strategies, regenerative therapies, brain-computer interface technologies, spinal cord electrical stimulation, and novel rehabilitation technologies, hold promise for altering the current limitations of SCI recovery. Ongoing research and innovation in these areas are crucial for driving a potential breakthrough in the prognosis of spinal cord injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- Treatment / Management -- Stem cell therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Stem cell treatments for spinal cord injuries can be categorized into 2 main types: supportive and regenerative therapies. Supportive stem cell therapy employs nonneural stem cells, such as mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) derived from bone marrow, umbilical cord, or adipose tissue. These cells are administered intravenously or intrathecally due to limited migration to the target area and differentiation into neural cells. MSCs release neurotrophic factors that can repair the injured area; however, their ability to replenish the nervous system is limited.

retrieved_docs.title: Central Cord Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The neuroanatomical–functional paradox observed among patients with central cord syndrome is due to the interplay between motor synergy encoding (MSE) neurons, as well as lesion-affected and recovery-related networks. [29] The neuronal plasticity, meta-inflammation, and diaschisis also have ripple effects. [29] Diffusion tensor imaging is a promising radiological armamentarium. [51] Induced pluripotent stem cell therapy might provide a newer therapeutic avenue in the near future. [55]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spine -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Vertebral development occurs through endochondral ossification. Cartilage forms around the 6th week of gestation and begins to be replaced by bone between weeks 7 and 8. Complete ossification is typically achieved in early adulthood by age 26, although bone remodeling, involving both degeneration and regeneration, continues throughout life.

retrieved_docs.title: Posttraumatic Syringomyelia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The syrinx cavity is typically lined by reactive astrocytes rather than a true epithelial lining, distinguishing it from congenital syringomyelia. The surrounding spinal cord tissue often exhibits a combination of demyelination, axonal loss, and necrosis, indicative of progressive damage due to chronic compression and ischemia. [14] Gliosis is a prominent feature, particularly at the margins of the syrinx, where reactive astrocytes form a glial scar. This scarring contributes to further obstruction of CSF flow and limits the potential for neural regeneration. The perisyringeal tissue often shows evidence of chronic inflammation, with infiltration of lymphocytes and macrophages. Microglial activation is frequently observed, further contributing to ongoing neuroinflammation and tissue degradation.

retrieved_docs.content: There is a laminar distribution of neurons in the gray matter, characterized by density and topography:

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- Evaluation -- Emerging Modalities
retrieved_docs.content: Quantitative MRI techniques, including magnetization transfer, magnetic resonance relaxation mapping, and diffusion imaging, aid in assessing the microstructural neuronal characteristics associated with myelination state and axonal degeneration or regeneration. A diffusion tensor tractography (DTT) reconstructs significant nerve bundles in 3-dimensional environments based on anisotropy features. DTT may be created from diffusion tensor imaging data using a computational technique. This modality can distinguish between damaged and deformed nerve fibers and healthy tissue. DTT provides information on the extent of SCI and may be used to plan surgical interventions. Implanting an epidural or intrathecal pressure-monitoring device may help clinicians better understand spinal cord perfusion pressure parameters following an injury. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- Treatment / Management -- Stem cell therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Most clinical trials focus on this therapy due to the easier MSC preparation and regulatory compliance. In contrast, loading therapy employs stem cells capable of producing neural cells, such as olfactory ensheathing cells and neural progenitor cells, and stem cells derived from embryonic stem cells. Engraftment of these cells has a greater likelihood of replacing lost nerve cells in terms of functionality, though the procedure requires invasive transplantation methods and complex cell preparation processes. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Spinal Cord -- Pathophysiology -- Spinal Cord Injury
retrieved_docs.content: Primary spinal cord injury occurs due to local spine deformation, such as direct compression. Secondary spinal cord injury occurs following primary injury and involves cascades of biochemical and cellular processes, including electrolyte disturbances, free radical damage, edema, ischemia, and inflammation. [25] Secondary spinal cord injury has several phases: acute, subacute, and chronic. During the acute phase (up to 48 hours after the primary injury), hemorrhage and ischemia lead to ion balance disruption, excitotoxicity, and inflammation. During the subacute phase (up to two weeks following primary injury), there is a phagocytic response and a reactive proliferation of astrocytes, which leads to a glial scar in the chronic phase. The thinking is that scarification is the critical component to permanent disability because it prevents axonal regeneration; axons otherwise could regenerate, but their growth is blocked. However, that notion has been challenged, and there are suggestions that astrocyte scar formation could aid in regeneration. [26] The scarification process is complete in the chronic phase (over six months after the primary injury). [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Spinal Cord -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The white matter is organized into tracts. Ascending tracts carry information from the sensory receptors to higher levels of the CNS, while descending tracts carry information from the CNS to the periphery. The major tracts and their most defining features are as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Spinal Cord -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The spinal cord is composed of gray and white matter, appearing in a cross-section as H-shaped gray matter surrounded by white matter. The gray matter consists of the cell bodies of motor and sensory neurons, interneurons, and neuropils (neuroglia cells and mostly unmyelinated axons). In contrast, the white matter is composed of interconnecting fiber tracts, which are primarily myelinated sensory and motor axons. The supports of the gray matter’s “H” make up the right dorsal, right ventral, left dorsal, and left ventral horns.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Corticospinal Cord Tract -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: During embryologic development, there is an overgrowth of axons distributed throughout the cortex, which incorporate into the corticospinal tract, and as development progresses, many of these axons are eliminated. Gray matter development begins a few weeks after the corticospinal tract axons reach the spinal cord. As growth continues, the corticospinal tract axons will reach the lower part of the cervical spinal cord by 24 weeks gestation. [3] [7] After birth, the corticospinal tract continues to develop. The tract is then refined, and motor control develops. The research proposes that refinement of the corticospinal tract happens through the elimination of transient termination and growth within the gray matter of the spinal cord.; this is followed by developing control of the corticospinal tract’s role in voluntary motor function. [2] The tract continues development through puberty, which is when the gender differences in white matter emerge. Studies have shown that androgens play a role in axonal development through the proliferation of neural cell bodies and the prevention of cell death following axonal injury. As a result, the development of white matter in males and females diverges during adolescence. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Central Nervous System -- Development -- Spinal Cord
retrieved_docs.content: The spinal cord is formed from the neural plate, now contains 3 layers: [3] [4] [3] Ventricular layer that lines the central canal Mantle layer that contains neuronal bodies, which will eventually form the gray matter Marginal layer that contains axons, and will eventually form the white matter

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Central Nervous System -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The spinal cord, formed from the caudal portion of the neural tube, is composed of gray and white matter. At 6 weeks of gestation, the gray matter aggregates, forming the dorsal alar and ventral basal plates. Interneurons form from the alar plate, while motor neurons form from the basal plate. Dorsal root ganglia, which brings information from the periphery to the spinal cord, arise for the neural crest cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Axonotmesis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Regarding the end organs, the remaining motor units on muscles increase in size with hypertrophy of the innervated muscle fibers. A pruning process later takes place when axons receiving inadequate neurotrophic factors diminish. This process begins within 4 days following the insult with healing requiring 3 to 6 months. [12] Pre-ganglionic avulsion injuries with the discontinuity of the spinal roots to the central cord lack regenerative neurons, unlike in postganglionic injuries distal to the proximal stump. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2898
retrieved_docs.content: Spinal cord injury is classified as an avulsion injury (تمزق جافوي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2194
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion is linked to تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage), and this tissue damage is further associated with سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity). Consequently, a cervical spine fracture that involves the spinal cord can lead to direct tissue injury that may produce neurotoxic effects, highlighting the importance of addressing both structural damage and its potential neurotoxic consequences in patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: The condition تمزق جافوي is closely associated with the dura mater, as the dura mater serves as the primary site where findings of تمزق جافوي are observed. In addition, تمزق جافوي functions as an antecedent or parent condition for spinal cord injury, indicating that the presence of تمزق جافوي may predispose patients to subsequent spinal cord injury. Recognizing the involvement of the dura mater in تمزق جافوي and its potential role in the development of spinal cord injury is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2307
retrieved_docs.content: The central nervous system (centrální nervový systém) is closely associated with the dura mater, reflecting an intimate anatomical relationship in which the dura mater envelops the CNS. The CNS is identified as the location where injuries to the central nervous system (إصابة الجهاز العصبي المركزي) are observed. The dura mater is specifically noted as the finding site of تمزق جافوي, a distinct lesion. This lesion, تمزق جافوي, is classified as a type of central nervous system injury (إصابة الجهاز العصبي المركزي) and is also regarded as the parent condition for spinal cord injury, indicating that spinal cord injury can be considered a subcategory or consequence of تمزق جافوي. Together, these relationships illustrate how the CNS, its protective covering, and related injuries such as تمزق جافوي and spinal cord injury are interconnected, emphasizing the importance of recognizing these links for accurate diagnosis and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: developmental disorders, and traumatic brain injury constitute an interconnected spectrum of nervous‑system disorders that inform comprehensive diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in neurology and neurosurgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Injury of central nervous system due to birth trauma is a condition in which the pathological finding is located within the central nervous system. The central nervous system is anatomically linked to the dura mater, as the dura mater constitutes a physical component of the protective meningeal layers that surround the brain and spinal cord. Consequently, injuries to the central nervous system, such as those caused by birth trauma, involve structures that are encased by the dura mater, underscoring the importance of the dura mater in shielding the central nervous system and influencing clinical assessment and management of such injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2896
retrieved_docs.content: تمزق جافوي is classified as a type of إصابة الجهاز العصبي المركزي (central nervous system injury). Within this hierarchy, spinal cord injury is considered a subcategory of تمزق جافوي, indicating that spinal cord injury falls under the broader category of تمزق جافوي and, consequently, is part of the spectrum of central nervous system injuries. This relationship clarifies that spinal cord injury is a specific manifestation within the larger group of injuries represented by تمزق جافوي, which itself is recognized as a central nervous system injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: The central nervous system (centrální nervový systém) is described as a physical component of the dura mater, indicating its anatomical relationship within the meningeal coverings. In clinical assessment, the central nervous system serves as the finding site for central nervous system injury (إصابة الجهاز العصبي المركزي), highlighting its relevance as the location where such pathological findings are identified. Understanding that the central nervous system is both anatomically linked to the dura mater and is the primary site of CNS injuries informs diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in neurology and neurosurgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2850
retrieved_docs.content: Transmission imaging is linked to صورة مقطعية محوسبة, indicating that this imaging modality encompasses computed tomography scans. In clinical practice, صورة مقطعية محوسبة frequently co‑occurs with صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية, reflecting that CT imaging is often performed together with spinal‑region myelographic or spinal cord imaging to provide complementary anatomical detail for diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1652
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion and the injury whose intent is undetermined but resulted from an aircraft crash are both described as having an associated morphology of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). The presence of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage) is further linked to سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), indicating that the tissue damage observed in these severe injuries may involve neurotoxic processes. This interconnected understanding highlights that both the cervical spine fracture with cord involvement and the ambiguous aircraft‑related injury share a common pathological feature of tissue damage, which can contribute to neurotoxic effects that are relevant for diagnosis, management, and prognosis.

This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

This delineation clarifies the overlap and distinctions between Exploration and Excision as unified yet separate surgical classifications within Clinical actions A‑G.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 982
retrieved_docs.content: The spinal disorder (اضطراب العمود الفقري) category includes post‑laminectomy syndrome (متلازمة ما بعد استئصال الصفيحة الفقرية) as a specific child condition, indicating that post‑laminectomy syndrome is a particular type of spinal disorder that can develop after surgical removal of the vertebral lamina.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 881
retrieved_docs.content: Laminectomy; excision intervertebral disc procedures are associated with the development of post‑laminectomy syndrome (متلازمة ما بعد استئصال الصفيحة الفقرية). Post‑laminectomy syndrome is classified as a subtype of spine disorder (اضطراب العمود الفقري). This relationship highlights that patients undergoing laminectomy or disc excision may experience persistent pain and functional impairment characteristic of this spinal disorder, underscoring the need for careful postoperative management and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 604
retrieved_docs.content: External cause codes: Firearm are classified as Injury due to legal intervention by firearm. Injury due to legal intervention by firearm is associated with the morphology of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion also has the associated morphology تَلَف الأنسجة, indicating that tissue damage is a common pathological feature of both firearm‑related legal intervention injuries and cervical spine fractures with spinal cord involvement. The tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة) is further linked to سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), suggesting that the morphological consequence of tissue injury may involve neurotoxic processes. Recognizing these connections helps clinicians understand injury mechanisms, anticipate tissue and neural complications, and guide appropriate patient care.
